meta.title: 1.2 Anatomy of a Component
meta.description: Every component follows the same pattern: data in, markup out.

blocks.prompt: You know that JSX is the markup language React uses. But where does the JSX live? Before reading this module, write down your best guess: what does the overall structure of a React component file look like?

blocks.answer: The user sees two lines of text: '$50' and '$40'. The calculations (basePrice, discount, finalPrice) happen in the logic zone above the return and are invisible to the user. Only the values inserted into the JSX via curly braces appear on screen. The className attributes apply CSS styling but are not visible as text.

options: A) A span that says 'active: 12 items'
B) A span that says 'status: count items'
C) A span that says '{status}: {count} items'
problems.explanation: The variables status ('active') and count (12) are defined in the logic zone above the return. Inside the JSX, {status} is replaced with 'active' and {count} is replaced with 12. The colon and the word 'items' are literal text. The className 'badge' is a CSS class and does not appear in the visible output.

options: A) The line 'const OrderSummary = () => {' -- everything after that is markup
B) The line 'return (' -- everything above is logic, everything inside is markup
C) The line 'const message = ...' -- that is the last piece of logic
problems.explanation: The return statement is always the boundary. Above it: variable declarations, calculations, and conditional logic (items, shipping, message). Inside it: the JSX that the user sees. The arrow function syntax (const OrderSummary = () => { ... }) is just a different way to write a function -- it does not change the logic/markup split.

options: A) It makes DashboardHeader the main component that other files can import and use
B) It deletes the helperFunction so only DashboardHeader exists
C) It sends the component to the server so it can be rendered
problems.explanation: export default marks DashboardHeader as the primary export of this file. Other files can import it with 'import DashboardHeader from ./DashboardHeader'. The helperFunction still exists in this file but is not exported -- it can only be used internally. export default does not involve servers or deletion.

blocks.prompt: Describe the structure of a React component in your own words. What are the two zones, and what goes in each one? Why does the function name need to start with a capital letter?

blocks.content: A React component is a function with a capitalized name that returns JSX. Everything above the return is logic and data preparation (invisible to the user). Everything inside the return is what the user sees. export default makes the component available to the rest of the app.

blocks.prompt: You now know that a component is a function that returns JSX, and that components have capitalized names. But what happens when one component uses another component inside its return statement? That is composition -- and it is how entire pages are built. Module 1.3 shows you how.

reviewQuestions.question: What is a React component?
options: A CSS file that styles the page
reviewQuestions.explanation: A React component is a JavaScript function that returns JSX -- the markup that describes what the user sees.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Spot on! A function that returns JSX -- that's the whole idea behind React components.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Not quite -- a React component is simply a function that returns JSX. Everything else builds on top of that core concept.

reviewQuestions.question: What does 'export default' do at the bottom of a React file?
options: It deletes all other functions in the file
It makes the component available for other files to import and use
It runs the component immediately when the app starts
reviewQuestions.explanation: export default marks the main component in the file so other files can import it and render it.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it! export default is how components become available to the rest of the app.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Almost! export default simply makes the component importable by other files -- it's how one component becomes usable in another.

reviewQuestions.question: In a React component, what is the 'logic zone'?
options: Everything above the return statement where variables and calculations live
The import statements at the top of the file
reviewQuestions.explanation: The logic zone is everything above the return statement -- variable declarations, calculations, and data preparation. The user never sees this directly.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Great job! The logic zone is where all the behind-the-scenes work happens before the JSX takes over.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Not this time -- the logic zone is everything above the return statement. That's where variables, calculations, and data prep happen before the markup.
